questions.to_find: Which digital logic block implements the given conditional assignment function

understand.plain: The function assigns y = a when x = 1, and y = b when x = 0. This is a classic selection operation where a control signal (x) chooses between two data inputs (a and b) to produce one output (y). This is exactly what a multiplexer does.

keywords.explain: A combinational circuit that selects one of several input signals and forwards it to a single output line based on a selection control signal.
keywords.example: A 2-to-1 MUX with select S, inputs I0 and I1: output = I0 when S=0, output = I1 when S=1.

keywords.explain: Assigning output based on a condition (like an if-else), which translates directly to a multiplexer in hardware.
keywords.example: if (sel) out = a; else out = b; maps to a 2-to-1 MUX.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 200' width='320' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='100' y='40' width='80' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>MUX</text><line x1='20' y1='75' x2='100' y2='75' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='10' y='72' class='sub ink'>a</text><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='100' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='10' y='127' class='sub ink'>b</text><line x1='140' y1='170' x2='140' y2='160' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='133' y='188' class='sub ink'>x</text><line x1='180' y1='100' x2='260' y2='100' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='265' y='104' class='sub ink'>y</text><text x='105' y='78' class='sub mut'>I1(a)</text><text x='105' y='133' class='sub mut'>I0(b)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 2-to-1 multiplexer with inputs a (I1) and b (I0), select signal x, and output y. When x=1, y=a; when x=0, y=b.

given.aim: Identify the digital block that implements y = a when x=1, y = b when x=0

terms.example: x=1 selects a; x=0 selects b
terms.connects: Acts as the select line of a MUX
These are the two data inputs to the MUX

terms.example: y is routed from either a or b based on x
terms.connects: This is the single output of the MUX

given.plan: Recognize that the if-else statement selects between two inputs based on a condition — this is exactly the operation performed by a 2-to-1 multiplexer. A full adder adds bits, a priority encoder generates a binary code for the highest-priority input, and a flip-flop is a sequential element, none of which match this function.

steps.title: Interpret the function as a selection operation
steps.apply: The function states: if x=1 then y=a, else y=b. Writing as Boolean: y = x.a + x'.b. This is the standard equation for a 2-to-1 MUX with select x, inputs a and b.
steps.note: This Boolean expression is identical to the output equation of a 2-to-1 multiplexer.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: Full adder: performs sum and carry of three bits — not a selection function. Priority encoder: encodes the position of the highest-priority active input — not this. Flip-flop: a sequential (memory) element — not combinational. Multiplexer: routes one of N inputs to output based on select lines — exactly matches y = x.a + x'.b.
steps.note: A 2-to-1 MUX directly implements any 2-input conditional assignment.

solution.result: C. Multiplexer — a 2-to-1 MUX with select x, data inputs a and b, perfectly implements the given function.

questions.formula_note: 2-to-1 Multiplexer output equation: Y = S'.I0 + S.I1, which directly maps to the given if-else conditional assignment.

questions.question:
Consider a circuit with two JK flip-flops connected such that the Q output of each flip-flop feeds back to its own J and K inputs (JK flip-flop configured as T flip-flop), with a clock input. The above circuit produces the output sequence:
A. 1111 1111 0000 0000
B. 1111 0000 1111 0000
C. 1010 1010 1010
D. 1111 0001 0101
questions.to_find: The output sequence produced by the given JK flip-flop circuit

understand.plain: A JK flip-flop where J=K=1 toggles its state on every clock edge. When two such flip-flops are cascaded (first flip-flop's Q feeds the clock of the second, or both toggle independently), the output pattern depends on the configuration. The most typical circuit shown produces an alternating toggle pattern at the output.

keywords.explain: When J=K=1, the flip-flop toggles (complements) its output on every active clock edge — acts as a T flip-flop (toggle flip-flop).
keywords.example: Q starts at 0; after each clock: 0 -> 1 -> 0 -> 1 -> ...

keywords.explain: The output alternates between 0 and 1 with each clock pulse when the flip-flop is in toggle mode.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 160' width='300' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='80' height='80' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>JK FF</text><text x='38' y='62' class='sub mut'>J</text><text x='38' y='102' class='sub mut'>K</text><rect x='180' y='40' width='80' height='80' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>JK FF</text><text x='188' y='62' class='sub mut'>J</text><text x='188' y='102' class='sub mut'>K</text><line x1='110' y1='60' x2='180' y2='60' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='138' y='55' class='sub ink'>Q1</text><line x1='260' y1='80' x2='290' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='265' y='75' class='sub ink'>Out</text><text x='70' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Clock</text><line x1='70' y1='120' x2='70' y2='135' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two JK flip-flops where Q of first feeds J and K of second, producing a divide-by-2 or alternating output sequence.

given.aim: Determine the periodic output sequence of the JK flip-flop circuit

terms.meaning: Acts as a toggle flip-flop — output complements on every clock edge
terms.connects: Sets the toggle behavior driving the output sequence

terms.meaning: The series of Q values observed over successive clock pulses
terms.connects: This is what we need to identify from the options

terms.meaning: The periodic input signal that triggers state transitions in flip-flops
terms.example: On each rising edge, a toggle FF changes state

given.plan: For a JK flip-flop with J=K connected to Q (or J=K=1), the flip-flop toggles on every clock pulse. Starting from Q=1, the output alternates 1,0,1,0,... giving the repeating pattern 1010. Option C matches this alternating toggle output.

steps.title: Analyze the JK flip-flop behavior
steps.formula_raw: Q(next) = J.Q' + K'.Q; for J=K=1: Q(next) = Q'
steps.apply: With J=K=1, the flip-flop toggles: Q(next) = Q'. Starting at Q=1: Clock 1 -> Q=0, Clock 2 -> Q=1, Clock 3 -> Q=0, ... Output: 1,0,1,0,1,0,...
steps.note: The toggle flip-flop produces an alternating binary sequence, which is the basis of binary counters.

steps.title: Match to the given options
steps.apply: The output sequence 1,0,1,0,1,0,... corresponds to option C: 1010 1010 1010. Options A and B show blocks of 1s and 0s (like ring counters or Johnson counters), and option D is irregular. Only C shows the correct toggle pattern.
steps.note: A single toggle flip-flop divides the clock frequency by 2, producing this 1010 pattern.

solution.result: C. 1010 1010 1010 — the JK flip-flop in toggle mode produces a continuously alternating output sequence.

questions.formula_note: JK flip-flop toggle mode: when J=K=1, Q(next) = Q', producing alternating 0/1 output — equivalent to a divide-by-2 counter.

questions.question:
Give a minimal DFA that performs as a mod-3 counter, i.e. outputs a 1 each time the number of 1's in the input sequence is a multiple of 3.

What is the minimum number of states required in such a DFA?
questions.to_find: Minimum number of states in a DFA that counts 1s modulo 3 and outputs 1 when count is a multiple of 3

understand.plain: We need a DFA (Deterministic Finite Automaton) that reads a binary input string and outputs 1 whenever the total number of 1s seen so far is divisible by 3 (i.e., 0, 3, 6, 9, ...). The DFA needs to track the count of 1s modulo 3, which can take values 0, 1, or 2 — requiring exactly 3 states.

keywords.explain: A computational model with a finite number of states, an input alphabet, transition function, start state, and accept states.
keywords.example: A 2-state DFA can recognize strings with even number of 1s.

keywords.explain: A counter that cycles through states 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, ... tracking the remainder when total 1s count is divided by 3.
keywords.example: After seeing three 1s, state returns to 0 (multiple of 3), so output = 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 380 180' width='380' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1.5' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='130' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>0</text><line x1='230' y1='82' x2='298' y2='82' stroke='#2C2C2A' understand.visual_alt: Three-state DFA: q0 (accept/output-1, count=0 mod 3), q1 (count=1 mod 3), q2 (count=2 mod 3). On input 1, transitions q0->q1->q2->q0. On input 0, self-loops at each state.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of DFA states to count 1s modulo 3 and output 1 at multiples of 3

terms.meaning: The remainder of dividing the number of 1s seen so far by 3; can be 0, 1, or 2
terms.example: After 4 ones: 4 mod 3 = 1, so we are in state q1
terms.connects: Each remainder value maps to exactly one DFA state

terms.meaning: The accepting/output state where total 1s seen is a multiple of 3
terms.example: After 0, 3, 6 ones, we are in q0 and output = 1
terms.connects: This is the initial and accepting state

terms.meaning: The DFA reads 0s and 1s; only 1s affect the counter state
terms.example: Reading 0 keeps the state unchanged; reading 1 advances to next state
terms.connects: Transitions on 1 cycle q0->q1->q2->q0; on 0, self-loops

given.plan: The DFA must track the number of 1s modulo 3. There are exactly 3 possible remainders (0, 1, 2), each needing its own state. The Myhill-Nerode theorem confirms 3 states are necessary and sufficient. State q0 is the start and accept state (output=1), with transitions on '1' cycling between states and '0' causing self-loops.

steps.title: Identify the equivalence classes
steps.formula_raw: States = {q_r : r = (number of 1s seen) mod 3}, r in {0, 1, 2}
steps.apply: The DFA must distinguish three equivalence classes of input histories: those where the count of 1s is 0 mod 3 (q0), 1 mod 3 (q1), and 2 mod 3 (q2). These are distinguishable because the additional number of 1s needed to reach the next multiple of 3 differs: 0 more for q0, 2 more for q1, 1 more for q2.
steps.note: By the Myhill-Nerode theorem, these 3 classes are pairwise distinguishable, so no DFA can use fewer than 3 states.

steps.title: Construct the minimal DFA
steps.apply: 3 states: q0 (start, accept), q1, q2. Transitions: on reading '1', move to next state cyclically (q0->q1, q1->q2, q2->q0). On reading '0', stay in current state. The DFA outputs 1 whenever it is in state q0.
steps.note: This DFA is minimal — it has exactly 3 states matching the 3 residues modulo 3.

solution.result: 3 states are required in the minimal DFA for a mod-3 counter.

questions.formula_note: Myhill-Nerode theorem: the minimum number of DFA states equals the number of equivalence classes of the Nerode relation; for mod-3 counting, there are 3 classes (residues 0, 1, 2).

questions.question:
For the synchronous counter shown in Fig, write the truth table of Q0, Q1, and Q2 after each clock pulse, starting from Q0 = Q1 = Q2 = 0 and determine the counting sequence and also the modulus of the counter.

The circuit uses three D/JK flip-flops with clock inputs connected together (synchronous). Starting state: Q0 = Q1 = Q2 = 0.

Note: Based on the circuit diagram, the flip-flops are interconnected with logic gates to form a synchronous up-counter. What is the modulus of this counter?
questions.to_find: The counting sequence and modulus of the synchronous counter starting from Q0=Q1=Q2=0

understand.plain: A synchronous counter has all flip-flops clocked simultaneously. With 3 flip-flops (Q0, Q1, Q2) starting at 000, and the standard synchronous binary counter configuration, the counter counts through binary values 0 through 7 and then returns to 0. The modulus is the total number of distinct states, which is 8 for a 3-bit binary counter.

keywords.explain: A counter where all flip-flops share the same clock input and change state simultaneously, unlike asynchronous (ripple) counters.
The total number of unique states (count values) the counter passes through in one complete cycle before repeating.
keywords.example: A mod-8 counter has 8 states: 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 then back to 0.

understand.visual_alt: Truth table for 3-bit synchronous counter: pulse 0 through 7 shows Q2 Q1 Q0 cycling from 000 to 111 then back to 000, with decimal values 0 to 7. Modulus = 8.

given.aim: Determine the counting sequence and modulus of a 3-flip-flop synchronous counter starting at Q0=Q1=Q2=0

terms.meaning: All flip-flops start at logic 0, corresponding to binary 000 = decimal 0
terms.example: This is the reset/cleared state of the counter
terms.connects: Starting point for the counting sequence

terms.meaning: Three flip-flops providing 3 bits of state, capable of representing 2^3 = 8 distinct states
terms.connects: Determines the maximum possible modulus is 8

terms.meaning: All flip-flops receive the same clock and update simultaneously, unlike ripple counters
terms.example: At each clock edge, all three Q outputs change together based on current state

given.plan: For a standard 3-bit synchronous binary up-counter: D0 = Q0', D1 = Q1 XOR Q0, D2 = Q2 XOR (Q1.Q0). Starting from 000, trace through 8 states: 000->001->010->011->100->101->110->111->000. The modulus is 8 since all 2^3 = 8 states are used before the sequence repeats.

steps.title: Trace the counting sequence
steps.formula_raw: D0=Q0', D1=Q1 XOR Q0, D2=Q2 XOR (Q1.Q0)
steps.apply:
Starting at Q2Q1Q0 = 000:
Pulse 1: 001 (decimal 1)
Pulse 2: 010 (decimal 2)
Pulse 3: 011 (decimal 3)
Pulse 4: 100 (decimal 4)
Pulse 5: 101 (decimal 5)
Pulse 6: 110 (decimal 6)
Pulse 7: 111 (decimal 7)
Pulse 8: 000 (decimal 0) -- returns to start
steps.note: The counter visits all 8 binary states in order before returning to 000.

steps.title: Determine the modulus
steps.formula_raw: Modulus = 2^n for an n-bit binary counter using all states
steps.apply: With n=3 flip-flops and using all possible states (000 through 111), the counter completes one full cycle in 8 clock pulses. Therefore, modulus = 8. The counting sequence is: 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6 -> 7 -> 0 (repeating).
steps.note: If the counter skipped any states, the modulus would be less than 8. Since all 8 states are visited, modulus = 8.

questions.formula_note: For a standard n-bit synchronous binary up-counter using all 2^n states, the modulus is 2^n. Excitation equations: D0=Q0', D1=Q1 XOR Q0, D2=Q2 XOR (Q1.Q0).
